en.extract:
The white-throated greenbul (Phyllastrephus albigularis) or white-throated bulbul, is a species of passerine bird in the bulbul family, Pycnonotidae.
It is widespread across the African tropical rainforest. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the Angola greenbul.


Taxonomy
The white-throated greenbul was formally described and illustrated in 1882 by the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe based on a specimen collected in the Fantee region of central Ghana. He coined the binomial name Xenocichla albigularis. It is now one of 21 species placed in the genus Phyllastrephus that was introduced in 1832 by the English naturalist William Swainson. The genus name combines Ancient Greek φυλλον/phullon  meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/strephō meaning "to toss" or "to turn". The specific epithet albigularis combines Latin albus meaning "white" with Modern Latin gularis meaning "throated". The Angola greenbul was formally considered to be a subspecies. The white-throated greenbul is now monophyletic: no subspecies are recognised.

The '''white-throated greenbul''' ('''''Phyllastrephus albigularis''''') or '''white-throated bulbul''', is a species of [[passerine]] bird in the [[bulbul]] family, [[Pycnonotidae]].
It is widespread across the [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|African tropical rainforest]]. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[Angola greenbul]].

==Taxonomy==
The white-throated greenbul was [[species description|formally described]] and illustrated in 1882 by the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] based on a specimen collected in the Fantee region of central Ghana. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Xenocichla albigularis''.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | author-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | date=1881 | publication-date=1882 | title=Catalogue of the Passeriformes or Perching Birds in the Collection of the British Museum. Cichlomorphae: Part III. Containing the first portion of the family Timeliidae (Babbling-Thrushes) | series=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum | volume=6 | location=London | publisher=Trustees of the British Museum | page=103, [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8306225 Plate 7] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8305893 }} Although the title page bears the date of 1881 this volume was not published until 1882.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=268-269 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/50585 }}</ref> It is now one of 21 species placed in the genus ''[[Phyllastrephus]]'' that was introduced in 1832 by the English naturalist [[William Swainson]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bulbuls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/bulbuls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 September 2024}}</ref> The genus name combines [[Ancient Greek]] φυλλον/''phullon '' meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/''strephō'' meaning "to toss" or "to turn".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Phyllastrephus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Phyllastrephus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=7 September 2024 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''albigularis'' combines [[Latin]] ''albus'' meaning "white" with [[Modern Latin]] ''gularis'' meaning "throated".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=albigularis | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=albigularis | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=7 September 2024 }}</ref> The [[Angola greenbul]] was formally considered to be a [[subspecies]]. The white-throated greenbul is now [[monophyletic]]: no subspecies are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>
